elements.sent-2411-001: Transitioning from quantum computation on physical qubits to quantum computation on encoded, logical qubits can improve the error rate of operations, and will be essential for realizing valuable quantum computational advantages.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using a neutral atom quantum processor with $256$ qubits, each an individual Ytterbium atom, we demonstrate the entanglement of $24$ logical qubits using the distance-two $\llbracket 4,2,2 \rrbracket$ code, simultaneously detecting errors and correcting for lost qubits.
elements.sent-2411-003: We also implement the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm with up to $28$ logical qubits encoded in the $\llbracket 4,1,2 \rrbracket$ code, showing better-than-physical error rates.
elements.sent-2411-004: We demonstrate fault-tolerant quantum computation in our approach, guided by the proposal of Gottesman (2016), by performing repeated loss correction for both structured and random circuits encoded in the $\llbracket 4,2,2 \rrbracket$ code.
elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, since distance-two codes can correct qubit loss, but not other errors, we show repeated loss and error correction using the distance-three $\llbracket 9,1,3 \rrbracket$ Bacon-Shor code.
elements.sent-2411-006: These results begin to clear a path for achieving scientific quantum advantage with a programmable neutral atom quantum~processor.
elements.sent-2411-007: Quantum computers can enable faster, more accurate solutions than classical computers.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, the biggest challenge to scaling up quantum computers is errors, or ``noise." Physical qubits and their operation are inherently noisy.
elements.sent-2411-009: State-of-the-art two-qubit gate error rates depend on the qubit implementation, and in some flagship quantum processors range, e.g., from $p = 0.1\%$ for an ion trap processor~\cite{Moses23quantinuum to an average of $p = 0.4\%$ for a superconducting qubit processor~\cite{google24surfacecode .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: If a circuit cannot tolerate errors, it can only run for roughly $1/p$ steps, or fewer, which is not nearly enough to achieve large-scale, advantageous computation.
elements.sent-2411-011: Even the best physical qubits are insufficiently reliable, without error correction, to run even moderate-size quantum algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-012: Indeed, obtaining a quantum advantage in scientific applications will likely require at least $100$ qubits with error rate $p$ at most $10^{-6}$, while a quantum advantage for industrial-scale problems may require upwards of $1000$ qubits with $p \sim 10^{-12}$~\cite{Beverland22quantumadvantage .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Such error rates will not be achievable at the physical level.
elements.sent-2411-014: Fortunately, encoding qubits in quantum error-correcting codes, thereby creating ``logical" qubits, should allow dramatic reductions in effective error rates.
elements.sent-2411-015: The operations must also be encoded, according to fault-tolerant constructions~\cite{AliferisGottesmanPreskill05 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus, by using quantum error correction and fault tolerance, noise in quantum hardware can be addressed through software, albeit with a cost in physical qubit overhead.
elements.sent-2411-017: In this work, we present a step toward achieving scientific quantum advantage by demonstrating reliable quantum computation using logical qubits that outperforms corresponding physical-qubit based computation.
elements.sent-2411-018: We present our results on a neutral atom quantum processor.
elements.sent-2411-019: Neutral atom quantum architectures recently have been in rapid development.
elements.sent-2411-020: Previously, neutral atom architectures were predominantly analog in nature.
elements.sent-2411-021: Recent progress has enabled neutral atom approaches to transition to digital, gate-based computation~\cite{evered2023high,scholl2023erasure,ma2023high,radnaev2024,Finkelstein2024,muniz2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The promise of neutral atoms as qubits is their configurability, both in count and connectivity, while also maintaining low error rates.
elements.sent-2411-023: The neutral atom quantum processor we use in this work has up to 256 qubits (Ytterbium atoms), with all-to-all connectivity enabled by atom movement, along with high two-qubit physical gate fidelity and atom loss detection.
elements.sent-2411-024: This allows for flexible computation, as well as the ability to both use and optimize over a wide range of quantum error-correcting codes, including those requiring non-local connectivity, for the underlying neutral atom architecture.
elements.sent-2411-025: We refer to the system for detecting and correcting errors, also during computation, as the qubit virtualization system, as encoded, logical qubits are virtual qubits made up of some number of physical qubits.
elements.sent-2411-026: Through co-optimization of fault-tolerant quantum error correction protocols within the qubit virtualization system and the neutral atom quantum processor design, we demonstrate the creation of logical qubits, their logical entanglement, and logical computation, while correcting for lost atoms.
elements.figure-2411-001: {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=.29]{images/cat24summary}}} \caption{ Summary of results for the $24$-qubit cat state. Shown is the error for measurements in the $X$ basis, the error for measurements in the $Z$ basis, and their sum, for three cases. A total less than $50\%$ implies entanglement. The baseline (left) is an unencoded $24$-qubit cat state; the error bars are large because we have conservatively chosen only the best run instead of averaging all baseline runs, which would further increase the separation between logical and physical performance. See \figref{f:cat24baselineruns} for more details. The encoded bars are based on different ways of interpreting the same data for $\llbracket4,2,2\rrbracket$-encoded cat states. In one case (middle), we reject trials with a detected error, but attempt to correct for lost qubits. In the other case (right), we reject trials with any lost qubit or detected error. } \label{f:cat24summary}
elements.figure-2411-002: {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=.3]{images/bvsummary}}} \caption{ Summary of results for the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm with an $n$-bit secret string. The string is always $1^n$, as the corresponding oracle is the most difficult to implement. The success probability is the probability of measuring $1^n$, conditioned on acceptance. For the unencoded algorithm (black), we accept when the $n$ measured atoms are present. For the encoded algorithm (red), we accept when all of the $n$ measured code blocks are decodable; in particular, it can detect faults and correct for lost atoms. The lighter points are based only on those trials with no initial atom loss. } \label{f:bvsummary}
elements.sent-2411-027: We demonstrate a variety of reliable quantum computations that outperform physical qubit computation using logical qubits co-designed with a programmable neutral atom quantum processor.
elements.sent-2411-028: First, we show entanglement of $24$ logical qubits in a ``cat'' state (\figref{f:cat24summary ).}
elements.sent-2411-029: This is the largest encoded cat state on record to date, illustrating the scale available in neutral atom processors.
elements.sent-2411-030: We then demonstrate quantum algorithms encoded into logical qubit computations.
elements.sent-2411-031: We implement the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm~\cite{BernsteinVazirani97complexity,cemm:qalg , a quantum algorithm well known for showing the higher efficiency of quantum computers versus classical.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Our implementations involve as many as $28$ logical qubits, encoded in $112$ physical qubits (\figref{f:bvsummary ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: Next, we demonstrate fault tolerance guided by a proposal of Gottesman~\cite{Gottesman16smallexperiments .}
elements.sent-2411-034: These experiments incorporate repeated error detection and, notably, repeated loss correction, a critical feature for scaling neutral atom processors.
elements.sent-2411-035: Each of the above experiments are encoded to distance two; they are capable of detecting phase and bit-flip errors and correcting atom loss.
elements.sent-2411-036: Finally, we demonstrate correcting both loss and errors, with repeated error correction in the distance-three Bacon-Shor code~\cite{Bacon05operator .. Related work.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Creating and verifying large cat states, also known as GHZ states~\cite{greenberg1989ghz , is a common benchmark for measuring hardware progress~\cite{Krenn24catstates .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The quality of an experiment is typically expressed by the fidelity with the ideal cat state, and where a fidelity greater than $1/2$ is the success criterion.
elements.sent-2411-039: For example, Moses et al.~\cite{Moses23quantinuum prepared the $20$-qubit cat state with an $86\%$ fidelity, and the $32$-qubit cat state with an $82\%$ fidelity, on the Quantinuum H2 trapped-ion processor.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Using superconducting qubits, Bao et al.~\cite{Bao24catstate60 prepared a $60$-qubit cat state with a $59\%$ fidelity.}
elements.sent-2411-041: For encoded qubits, the published record to date is an encoded $12$-qubit cat state with at least a $99.8\%$ fidelity, also on H2~\cite{ReichardtMicrosoft24tesseract .}
elements.sent-2411-042: On a neutral atom system, Bluvstein et al.~\cite{BluvsteinHarvard23neutralatoms have prepared a four-qubit encoded cat state with a fidelity of $72(2)\%$ using error correction, ranging to $99.85^{+0.1 _{-1.0}\%$ using full error detection and therefore with much lower acceptance probability.}}

elements.sent-2411-043: The Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm for finding a secret string is another benchmark for quantum processors~\cite{Lubinski24qedcbenchmarks .}
elements.sent-2411-044: It has been implemented with unencoded qubits on superconducting processors~\cite{Roy20bernsteinvazirani, DahlhauserHumble20bernsteinvazirani, PokharelLidar23bernsteinvazirani , ion trap processors~\cite{FallekBrown16bernsteinvazirani, DebnathMonroe16iontrap, Wright19iontrap11benchmark , and both processor types~\cite{Linke17bernsteinvaziraniiontrapsuperconducting, Murali19bernsteinvazirani, BlinovWuMonroe21bernsteinvaziraniiontrapsuperconducting, Lubinski21algorithmbenchmarks , for various lengths of the secret string, up to length $26$ for superconducting qubits~\cite{PokharelLidar23bernsteinvazirani and up to length $20$ for ion traps~\cite{Lubinski21algorithmbenchmarks .}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-049: A critical capability for enabling more complex, reliable computation in a quantum processor is repeated detection and correction of errors.
elements.sent-2411-050: The latest generations of quantum hardware have enabled a variety of such demonstrations within the past few years.
elements.sent-2411-051: Repeated error detection with distance-two codes has been demonstrated on ion traps, including three rounds on eight logical qubits~\cite{self22icebergcode , and 15 rounds on four logical qubits~\cite{Yamamoto23phaseestimattionerrordetection .}}
elements.sent-2411-052: Demonstrations based on Gottesman's protocol~\cite{Gottesman16smallexperiments for fault tolerance with the $\llbracket 4,2,2 \rrbracket$ code, but without including repeated error detection, include~\cite{Willsch_2018, Harper19edd,Vuillot_2018,Linke17edexp,Cane2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: Repeated error correction, with higher-distance codes, has also been demonstrated.
elements.sent-2411-054: For single logical qubits, demonstrations include those on trapped-ion processors~\cite{honeywell21steane, Postler23steaneec and superconducting processors~\cite{Krinner21repeatedsurfaceec,google23surface,google24surfacecode, amazon24catec each with varying numbers of rounds, e.g., up to nearly $36$ rounds of error correction ($250$ syndrome extraction cycles) on the $\llbracket49,1,7\rrbracket$ surface code, with a $0.143(3)\%$ logical error rate per cycle~\cite{google24surfacecode .}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Repeated error correction on multiple qubits has been implemented, including three rounds on two logical qubits~\cite{Silva24microsoft12qubitcode , and five rounds on eight logical qubits~\cite{ReichardtMicrosoft24tesseract , each while beating error rates of physical baselines.}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{images/architecture} \caption{ Diagram of experimental architecture. $^{171}$Yb atoms, which each encode a physical qubit in their ground-state nuclear spin, can occupy tweezer traps within either a register array or an interaction zone (IZ). Up to 8 pairs of atoms interact at a time in the IZ, driven by two-qubit (2Q) gate lasers to implement controlled phase gates on the atoms in each pair. Arbitrary single-qubit (1Q) operations can be applied in parallel to the atoms within a row of the register; here one atom is missing. Each atom has a home register location, indicated by dashed lines, where it is originally loaded and where 1Q gates can be applied. Movement of atoms between the two zones enables arbitrary connectivity for 2Q gates. Up to eight atoms from the same row can move in parallel, but their paths cannot cross. } \label{f:system}
elements.sent-2411-056: All results presented in this work were obtained on a quantum processor based on reconfigurable arrays of neutral $^{171}$Yb atoms, depicted in \figref{f:system , with the qubits encoded in the ground-nuclear spin states ($^1$S$_0$, $m_F = \pm 1/2$).}
elements.sent-2411-057: Aspects of the system have previously been described in~\cite{barnes2022assembly, norcia2023midcircuit, norcia2024iterative , as well as an accompanying publication~\cite{muniz2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Further system and benchmarking details will follow in a separate manuscript, in preparation.
elements.sent-2411-059: By moving atoms between arrays of static optical tweezer traps using mobile tweezers, this platform enables arbitrary connectivity between qubits, for a large numbers of qubits.
elements.sent-2411-060: Single-qubit operations and qubit storage are performed in a register array, which is loaded in an iterative manner from a repeatedly filled reservoir~\cite{norcia2024iterative .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Two-qubit gates are performed in a separate interaction zone (IZ).
elements.sent-2411-062: For this work, up to 256 atoms are available for computation, and up to eight atoms within the same row may be moved between the register and IZ at a time, subject to constraints associated with intermodulation in the acousto-optic deflectors used to generate the moving tweezers.
elements.sent-2411-063: Typical atom loss probabilities per move are below 0.1\%.
elements.sent-2411-064: State-resolved, low-loss readout is performed by transferring atoms from the tweezer arrays into traps formed by cavity-enhanced optical lattices, which allows for state and atom detection with error and loss rates at the few parts-per-thousand level (see \cite{norcia2024iterative for a detailed characterization of state preparation and imaging).}
elements.sent-2411-065: Importantly, the ability to perform state-selective, low-loss imaging allows us to distinguish between different types of error.
elements.sent-2411-066: An image performed prior to circuit execution allows the identification of sites successfully occupied by atoms, while a pair of images directly after circuit execution and after optical pumping within the qubit subspace allow determination of both the presence and state of the atoms.
elements.sent-2411-067: In the encoded Bernstein-Vazirani experiment with $n = 7$, for example, on average $0.52(7)\%$ of the $32$ atoms were not loaded, and $1054$ of $1200$ shots, or $85(2)\%$, had no initial loss.
elements.sent-2411-068: The fraction of qubits lost during the circuit averaged $2.3^{+0.2 _{-0.1}\%$.}

elements.sent-2411-070: Single-qubit operations are performed in parallel on rows of atoms within the register, with the pulse area, phase, and presence of the gates controlled individually on each site using an architecture similar to that described in Ref.~\cite{barnes2022assembly .}
elements.sent-2411-071: These gates are performed using two-photon Raman transitions driven by two independently controlled and site-resolved laser beams detuned roughly 10~GHz from the $^3$P$_1$, $F=1/2$ manifold.
elements.sent-2411-072: We calibrate an $S_X$ gate on each site of the register, and perform virtual $Z$ rotations of arbitrary angle by changing the relative phase of the two drive lasers on subsequent gates.
elements.sent-2411-073: We characterize the performance of the single-qubit operations using a Clifford randomized benchmark sequence.
elements.sent-2411-074: At depths of up to 500 Clifford gates, we find an average fidelity of 99.85(2)\% per Clifford gate, averaged across three rows of the register spanning those used in this work.
elements.sent-2411-075: Because the nuclear spin qubit is well-isolated from differential light-shifts and has low sensitivity to magnetic fields, we do not automatically apply dynamical decoupling sequences for the circuits presented in this work.
elements.sent-2411-076: Two-qubit controlled-phase (CZ) gates are performed in the IZ on up to eight pairs of atoms in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-077: These gates are mediated by excitation to a highly-excited Rydberg state ($65^3\mathrm{S _1$).}
elements.sent-2411-078: The sites of the IZ are spaced such that atoms occupying the same pair of sites are in the so-called ``Rydberg blockade" limit, where the strength of interactions between the atoms exceeds other relevant energy scales, while atoms in different pairs interact weakly enough to be treated independently.
elements.sent-2411-079: All operations in the IZ are applied in parallel to all atoms within the IZ.
elements.sent-2411-080: Atoms are excited to the Rydberg state using a sequential two-photon transition.
elements.sent-2411-081: State-selectivity is provided by the first stage of the two-photon transition, from $^1$S$_0$, $m_F = -1/2$ to the metastable $^3$P$_0$, $m_F = 1/2$ state.
elements.sent-2411-082: Polarization selectivity, along with the narrow linewidth of the transition and slow Rabi rate (10~kHz) relative to the qubit frequency (376~kHz) ensure that only one qubit state is excited to the metastable state.
elements.sent-2411-083: From the metastable state, atoms are excited to the Rydberg state using a UV laser pulse (total duration 110~ns) that implements a gate similar to the time-optimal controlled-phase (CZ) gate described in \cite{jandura2022time .}
elements.sent-2411-084: While the IZ traps are equally confining for the ground and metastable states, the register traps are near a tune-out wavelength for the metastable states, providing near-zero trapping potential.
elements.sent-2411-085: Thus, any gate errors that lead to population being left in the metastable state (such as some coherent population errors on either stage of the two-photon transition) are converted to loss and can be detected.
elements.sent-2411-086: This ``erasure conversion" proves useful in identifying the location of errors \cite{wu2022erasure .}
elements.sent-2411-087: We quantify the performance of our two-qubit gates using several variations on randomized benchmarking.

elements.sent-2411-089: To isolate the performance of the two-qubit gates themselves, in the absence of any errors that may be incurred through atomic movement, we perform interleaved randomized benchmarking (IRB) on static atoms within the IZ.

elements.sent-2411-140: Consider a black box oracle $\{0,1\}^n\rightarrow\{0,1\}$, with a secret string $s \in \{0,1\}^n$, that on query $x$ returns $s \cdot x = s_1 x_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus s_n x_n$, with $\oplus$ denoting addition modulo~$2$.
elements.sent-2411-141: Classically, $n$ queries to the oracle are required and sufficient to learn~$s$.
elements.sent-2411-142: The Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm~\cite{BernsteinVazirani97complexity,cemm:qalg makes a single query to the quantum version of this oracle, $U_s = \sum_{x \in \{0,1\}^n, b \in \{0,1\} \ketbra{b \oplus (s \cdot x) {b \otimes \ketbra{x {x}$, to learn~$s$ through the following steps: \begin{equation}\ket{-, +^n} \overset{U_s} \longmapsto \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2^n}} \sum_{x\in\{0,1\}^n}{(-1)^{s\cdot x}\ket{-, x}} \overset{I \otimes H^{\otimes n}}\longmapsto \ket{-, s} \enspace .\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-143: This classic algorithm was designed to prove an oracle separation between the classical randomized and quantum polynomial-time complexity classes $\BPP$ and $\BQP$.
elements.sent-2411-144: For $n \in \{7, 11, 15, 19, 23, 27\}$, we implement the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm for the hardest secret string, $s = 1^n$.
elements.sent-2411-145: We implement the algorithm using $\llbracket 4,1,2 \rrbracket$-encoded logical qubits, as well as corresponding unencoded baseline circuits.
elements.sent-2411-146: Figure~\ref{f:bv20atomarrangement shows the encoded circuit for $n = 19$, involving $n+1 = 20$ logical qubits, $4(n+1) = 80$ atoms and $6n+2 = 116$ physical CZ gates.}

elements.sent-2411-155: Computing the success probability conditioned on acceptance makes sense if we treat the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm as a generic quantum algorithm, as done above.
elements.sent-2411-156: However, it was originally proposed as a quantum query algorithm, in which minimizing the number of oracle queries was of key importance.
elements.sent-2411-157: In this light, we can look at the data and ask whether with only one oracle query allowed (one trial), the encoded circuit still beats the unencoded circuit.
elements.sent-2411-158: In short, the answer is probably not.
elements.sent-2411-159: In this case, neither the encoded nor unencoded algorithms can condition on acceptance, and with only one trial it requires guessing the secret string.

elements.section-2411-006: Repeated loss correction and transversal operations for the $\llbracket 4,2,2\rrbracket$~code
elements.subsection-2411-001: Repeated loss correction with CZ and dual CZ logical gates
elements.sent-2411-170: The circuits considered so far have only used loss correction and error detection at the very end, when decoding the final measurements.
elements.sent-2411-171: However, it's important to be able to detect and correct errors between computational steps of an algorithm, to enable deeper computation.
elements.sent-2411-172: To that end, we explore using loss correction in the middle of a larger circuit, demonstrating repeated fault-tolerant loss correction.
elements.sent-2411-173: In order to make the circuits more interesting, between rounds of loss correction we apply some of the fault-tolerant logical operations given in \secref{s:fourqubitcodes .}
elements.sent-2411-174: Figure~\ref{f:xxxxandzzzzsyndrome gives a circuit for fault-tolerant error detection for the $\llbracket 4,2,2 \rrbracket$ code.}
elements.sent-2411-175: It measures the stabilizer generators $X^{\otimes 4}$ and $Z^{\otimes 4}$ in parallel, with one measurement outcome flagging the other for possible correlated errors.
elements.sent-2411-176: Furthermore, the circuit in \figref{f:xxxxandzzzzsyndromeerasure replaces two of the data qubits with fresh qubits, so the full data block can be refreshed every two rounds of error detection.}
elements.sent-2411-177: This allows us to use the $\llbracket 4,2,2 \rrbracket$ code to correct a lost qubit during a computation, not just at the very end as in our logical cat state procedure.
elements.figure-2411-017: \subfigure[] {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=1.2]{images/zzzzsyndromenonft}}} \subfigure[\label{f:xxxxandzzzzsyndrome-no-erasure}] {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=1.2]{images/xxxxandzzzzsyndrome}}} \subfigure[\label{f:xxxxandzzzzsyndromeerasure}] {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=1.2]{images/xxxxandzzzzsyndromeerasure}}} \caption{ The top circuit (a) measures the parity of four qubits, i.e., the syndrome of $ZZZZ$. However, it is not fault tolerant; a single $Z$ fault on the ancilla can spread undetected to a weight-two $Z$ error. The next circuit (b) remedies this, and simultaneously measures $XXXX$, using a trick from~\cite{Reichardt18steane}. Now the bad $Z$ fault will be detected, or ``flagged," by the $XXXX$ measurement, and similarly the $ZZZZ$ measurement will flag a bad $X$ fault. This circuit gives an efficient, fault-tolerant error detection procedure for the $\llbracket 4,2,2 \rrbracket$ code. However, it does not correct for a lost qubit. The bottom circuit (c) fixes this, halfway, by switching the direction of the final two CNOT gates. (This is equivalent to inserting swap-based ``leakage-detection units"~\cite{Chow24leakagedetectionunit}.) The last two data qubits are replaced with fresh ancillas, so in particular if one of them is lost it will be replaced. Thus we can replace all four qubits in two rounds of error detection. Future work will investigate this paradigm by combining mid-circuit measurement, qubit reuse and reinitialization, along with periodic atom cooling to remove a fundamental obstacle to neutral atom quantum computing by limiting the depth of circuit any atom must undergo. } \label{f:xxxxandzzzzsyndrome}

elements.section-2411-007: Repeated error correction with the distance-three Bacon-Shor code
elements.sent-2411-207: Running deep logical circuits in a neutral atom quantum computer will ultimately require repeated correction of both errors within the qubit subspace and atom loss from erasure conversion.
elements.sent-2411-208: In order to demonstrate this, we choose the distance-three $\llbracket 9,1,3\rrbracket$ Bacon-Shor code.
elements.sent-2411-209: This code has previously been implemented on trapped-ion systems~\cite{Egan21iontrapbaconshor, Huang2024iontrapbaconshorsteaneshor .}
elements.sent-2411-210: A code distance greater than two is needed to correct errors other than loss.
elements.sent-2411-211: To our knowledge, the present work is the first demonstration of repeated error correction in neutral atoms.
elements.sent-2411-212: We employ a scheme similar to that of Steane and Knill~\cite{steane97,Knill05,Silva24microsoft12qubitcode , leveraging its ability to correct atom loss; see \figref{f:steaneerrorcorrection .}}
elements.sent-2411-213: In this work, the ancilla qubits are held until readout at the end of the circuit, so each round of error correction requires the use of additional qubits.
elements.sent-2411-214: However, in principle, this scheme can be combined with mid-circuit measurement and reset where readout and loss detection, combined with mid-circuit reloading, will enable arbitrary-depth circuits.
elements.figure-2411-023: \subfigure[] {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=.6]{images/steaneec}}} $\qquad$ \subfigure[\label{f:steaneecmodified}] {\includegraphics[scale=.6]{images/steaneecmodified}} \caption{ (a) In Steane's scheme to correct $X$ errors, an ancilla code block is fault-tolerantly prepared in the encoded $\ket{+}$ state. The data block is coupled to it with transversal CNOT gates into the ancillas. This logical CNOT has no logical effect, since $\ket{+}$ is preserved by $X$ (NOT), but it copies $X$ errors to the ancilla block. Measuring the ancillas transversally in the $Z$ basis allows the $X$ errors to be identified and corrected. (Generally, the corrections are not actually applied to the data block, but rather stored classically, as the ``Pauli frame" for the data.) $Z$ error correction is symmetrical, using an ancilla block prepared as encoded $\ket{0}$. (b) We modify the scheme so that $X$ and $Z$ error correction are both implemented by logical one-qubit teleportation~\cite{Zhou00onebitteleportation}, thus making a connection to teleportation-based syndrome extraction~\cite{Knill05,Silva24microsoft12qubitcode}. This method has the advantage that a lost atom will be refreshed with a new qubit. The logical corrections to complete one-qubit teleportation are not shown above, but are stored in the Pauli frame. } \label{f:steaneerrorcorrection}
elements.sent-2411-215: After one round of error correction, we find a logical error rate of $4.9^{+1.0 _{-0.9}\%$ and after two rounds the error rate is $8^{+2 _{-1}\%$.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: While these results suggest the opportunity to further improve physical error rates, they also serve as a demonstration of the mechanism to correct for both errors and loss.
elements.sent-2411-217: This can be seen in the analysis of the successful trials with one round of error correction, where on average $0.20$ erasures and at least $0.14$ errors were corrected in the readout of the final data block, whereas $0.40$ erasures and $0.50$ errors were corrected in the previous round of $X$ and $Z$ error correction, a higher average number of corrections.
elements.sent-2411-218: In the successful trials with two rounds of error correction, on average $0.22$ erasures and at least $0.13$ errors were corrected in the readout of the final data block (consistent with the one-round data), while $0.95$ erasures and $0.88$ errors were corrected in the previous error-correction rounds.
elements.sent-2411-219: In order to compare encoded and physical results, we must choose a meaningful physical baseline.
elements.sent-2411-220: From the standpoint of the encoded data, the logical circuit is a quantum memory, applying the logical identity operation.
elements.sent-2411-221: For a meaningful comparison, we show results for repeated teleportation of physical qubit states, following the pattern of repeated teleportation of the logical data in \figref{f:steaneecmodified .}

elements.sent-2411-222: In this work, we have created large entangled logical states, implemented computational algorithms on encoded qubits, demonstrated qubit loss correction, and implemented a code with the capacity for repeated loss and error correction.
elements.sent-2411-223: Altogether, these results highlight the unique features and promise of neutral atoms as a platform for reliable fault-tolerant quantum computing.
elements.sent-2411-224: The large encoded cat states and the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithms are enabled by the combination of scalability, high-fidelity operations inherent to neutral atoms, and all-to-all connectivity among the qubits.
elements.sent-2411-225: For larger-scale logical computation, this connectivity paired with high qubit count will enable the use of nonlocal error-correcting codes with high encoding rates and low physical qubit overhead \cite{xu2024fastparallelizablelogicalcomputation .}
elements.sent-2411-226: All of the encoded experiments, particularly those with repeated error detection, highlight the usefulness of loss-to-erasure conversion in the context of encoded qubits.
elements.sent-2411-227: They demonstrate that logical circuits can operate effectively with atom loss, which will be a key feature of large-scale neutral atom quantum computers.
elements.sent-2411-228: This work and similar work with other neutral atom platforms \cite{BluvsteinHarvard23neutralatoms , as well as recent work with superconducting qubits~\cite{google24surfacecode and trapped ion qubits~\cite{Silva24microsoft12qubitcode,ReichardtMicrosoft24tesseract , highlight a critical advance in the field of quantum computation: the transition from scientific results computed with physical qubits to computational advances achieved with logical qubits.}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: After a relatively short time the neutral atom platform has shown significant advancement from its inception as an experimental quantum computing platform.
elements.sent-2411-230: In the near future we expect advances in both 2Q gate fidelities as more iterations are performed to reduce gate laser noise as well as significant scale up towards $\num{10000}$ qubits allowing for larger distance codes, more performant logical qubits, and more impressive demonstrations of fault-tolerant, universal quantum computation.
elements.sent-2411-231: The combination of a hardware-optimized qubit virtualization system, complete with high-rate and high-efficiency quantum error correction, with a programmable neutral atom quantum processor, inclusive of mid-circuit measurement~\cite{norcia2023midcircuit , continuous atom reloading~\cite{norcia2024iterative , and increased two-qubit gate fidelity, is a promising path to achieving large-depth, logical computation for scientific quantum advantage.}}
elements.sent-2411-232: We thank Zulfi Alam, David Bohn, Jeongwan Haah, Chetan Nayak, Pasi Kostamo, Vadym Kliuchnikov, Dennis Tom, Matthias Troyer, and Jason Zander for their support and feedback during this project.. \ifx\compilefullpaper\undefined \bibliographystyle{halpha-abbrv.}
elements.sent-2411-233: Quantum computing experiments are transitioning from running on physical qubits to using encoded, logical qubits.
elements.sent-2411-234: Fault-tolerant computation can identify and correct errors, and has the potential to enable the dramatically reduced logical error rates required for valuable algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-235: However, it requires flexible control of high-fidelity operations performed on large numbers of qubits.
elements.sent-2411-236: We demonstrate fault-tolerant quantum computation on a quantum processor with $256$ qubits, each an individual neutral Ytterbium atom.
elements.sent-2411-237: The operations are designed so that key error sources convert to atom loss, which can be detected by imaging.
elements.sent-2411-238: Full connectivity is enabled by atom movement.
elements.sent-2411-239: We demonstrate the entanglement of $24$ logical qubits encoded into $48$ atoms, at once catching errors and {correcting for, on average 1.8, lost atoms.}
elements.sent-2411-240: We also implement the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm with up to $28$ logical qubits encoded into $112$ atoms, showing better-than-physical error rates.
elements.sent-2411-241: In both cases, ``erasure conversion," changing errors into a form that can be detected independently from qubit state, improves circuit performance.
elements.sent-2411-242: These results begin to clear a path for achieving scientific quantum advantage with a programmable neutral atom quantum processor.
elements.sent-2411-243: Quantum computers are potentially powerful, but are challenging to control precisely.
elements.sent-2411-244: State-of-the-art two-qubit gate error rates in some flagship quantum processors range from $0.1\%$ for a trapped-ion device~\cite{Moses23quantinuum to an average of $0.4\%$ for a superconducting qubit device~\cite{google24surfacecode .}}
elements.sent-2411-245: Moderate-size quantum algorithms require much lower error rates, below $10^{-6}$~\cite{Beverland22quantumadvantage .}
elements.sent-2411-246: This precision can likely only be achieved using quantum error-correcting codes to store and fault-tolerantly operate on ``logical" qubits~\cite{AliferisGottesmanPreskill05 .}
elements.sent-2411-247: Here, we advance the shift from physical to logical qubits, by demonstrating fault-tolerant, encoded computations on logical qubits.
elements.sent-2411-248: The encoded circuits outperform unencoded circuits on physical qubits.
elements.sent-2411-249: Our results are achieved using a neutral atom quantum processor with $256$ qubits, Ytterbium atoms (\figref{f:system ), designed to enable loss conversion and detection for improved logical performance.}
elements.sent-2411-250: Neutral atom devices have been mainly analog, but recent progress has enabled digital, gate-based computation~\cite{evered2023high,scholl2023erasure,ma2023high,radnaev2024,Finkelstein2024,muniz2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-251: The processor here has low gate error, and atom movement enables all-to-all connectivity for flexible computation and logical operation design.
elements.sent-2411-252: The physical two-qubit gates are designed so that key error sources convert to atom loss, which imaging has been designed to detect.
elements.sent-2411-253: With unencoded circuits, data with lost atoms can be discarded, and using this selection we prepare an entangled $40$-qubit ``cat" state (\figref{f:ghz ).}
elements.sent-2411-254: Loss conversion is even more advantageous operating on encoded data, though; a distance-two code can detect errors and correct for lost atoms.
elements.sent-2411-255: We demonstrate the creation and entanglement of logical qubits, and logical computation.
elements.sent-2411-256: We prepare a cat state on $24$ logical qubits, encoded in $48$ atoms (\figref{f:cat24summary ).}
elements.sent-2411-257: We then demonstrate a quantum algorithm on logical qubits.
elements.sent-2411-258: We fault-tolerantly run the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm~\cite{BernsteinVazirani97complexity,cemm:qalg , well known for showing the higher efficiency of quantum computers versus classical, on as many as $28$ logical qubits, encoded in $112$ atoms (\figref{f:bvsummary ), with better-than-physical results.}}
elements.sent-2411-259: This illustrates the scale available in neutral atom processors, and their promise as reliable, fault-tolerant quantum processors.
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[scale=.17]{images/architecture2.pdf} \caption{ Experimental architecture. {\bf a}, {\bf b}, $^{171}$Yb atoms, each giving a physical qubit in the ground-state nuclear spin, can occupy tweezer traps within either a register array or an interaction zone (IZ). Up to 8 pairs of atoms interact at a time in the IZ, driven by two-qubit (2Q) gate lasers to implement controlled-phase (CZ) gates. These lasers perform coherent sequential excitation to a metastable clock state ($^{3}$P${_0}$) and the Rydberg state (65$^{3}$S${_1}$). Arbitrary single-qubit (1Q) operations can be applied in parallel to the atoms within a register row using Raman transitions detuned from the $^{3}$P${_1}$ manifold. Moving atoms between the zones enables arbitrary 2Q gate connectivity. {\bf c}, Circuit-running sequence. Dissipative operations (left column) are performed with atoms held in a cavity-enhanced optical lattice. Three images (orange boxes) allow us to determine initial occupancy of register sites, as well as the final state and presence of atoms. Coherent operations (right column) are performed with atoms held in tweezers. } \label{f:system}

elements.figure-2411-028: {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=.3]{images/bvsummary}}} \caption{ Results for the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm with an $n$-bit secret string. elements.sent-2411-346: We load atoms into the register using the methods described in \cite{norcia2024iterative , providing more than enough qubits for the circuit (typically filling 320 register sites to approximately 99\% filling fraction over up to 14 loading cycles).}

elements.sent-2411-349: By loading more register sites than are used in a given circuit, we can improve data rates by reusing atoms in multiple circuit instances.
elements.sent-2411-350: To do this, we determine which atoms were lost in a circuit, and refill those sites using extra atoms, then perform the rest of our state preparation protocol (described in the following section).
elements.sent-2411-351: This can be repeated as long as atoms remain in the register.
elements.sent-2411-352: Our platform benefits from the ability to determine successful loading of our desired qubit array before circuit execution, and to separately determine the survival and final state of an atom after the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-353: We achieve this by performing three images per circuit instance, in addition to images used to initially arrange atoms into the qubit register.
elements.sent-2411-354: A first ``preselect" image determines whether the atoms were successfully loaded into the register before executing the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-355: A second ``readout image" comprises the state readout of the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-356: A third ``survival" image determines whether an atom is still present after the circuit.
elements.sent-2411-357: In all images, we measure the presence of an atom in state $\ket{1}$.
elements.sent-2411-358: The pre-select and survival images are combined with optical pumping into $\ket{1}$, so only determine the site occupancy.
elements.sent-2411-359: In the readout image, the optical pumping is omitted and state selectivity is maintained.
elements.sent-2411-360: Following the preselect image, we perform gray-molasses cooling and optically pump into state $\ket{0}$.
elements.sent-2411-361: Aspects of our imaging procedure have been described in \cite{norcia2023midcircuit, norcia2024iterative .}
elements.sent-2411-362: The imaging is performed in a cavity-enhanced 784~nm wavelength 3D lattice by collecting the photons scattered from the cycling transition from $\ket{1}$ to $^3P_1 \ket{3/2, 3/2}$.
elements.sent-2411-363: Optical pumping and gray-molasses cooling are also performed in the cavity-enhanced lattice.
elements.sent-2411-364: Between imaging and circuit execution, atoms are transferred between the lattice and register tweezers.
elements.sent-2411-365: Typical classification errors, loss probabilities, and spin-flip probabilities for the imaging are provided in~\cite{norcia2024iterative .}

elements.sent-2411-371: Arbitrary gate connectivity in our system is obtained by moving selected atoms from the register into an interaction zone (IZ) where two-qubit gates are performed (see \figref{f:system ).}
elements.sent-2411-372: Movement is performed using a set of tweezers generated by a pair of crossed AODs.
elements.sent-2411-373: Atoms within a single row can be moved in parallel, provided that the move profile does not involve parasitic tweezer spots generated by intermodulation tones crossing the position of the intentional tweezers used for movement.
elements.sent-2411-374: In practice, we guarantee this condition by requiring parallel moves of more than two atoms to be shape-preserving (stretching and translation of the tweezer locations only).
elements.sent-2411-375: Moves are broken up into straight segments, with the movement velocity along each segment following a sinusoidal profile, with a total duration scaled to maintain a fixed peak acceleration (as the square root of the length of the segment).
elements.sent-2411-376: Move durations are $0.12 \sqrt{L}$~ms, where $L$ is the length of the move in units of the 3.3~$\mu$m grid spacing of the register.
elements.sent-2411-377: Atoms are handed between static and rearrangement tweezers by ramping the depth of the rearrangement tweezers over 0.4~Ms. For up to 50 moves, we observe a typical loss probability of $5(1) \times 10^{-4}$.

elements.subsection-2411-009: Repeated loss correction with CZ logical gates
elements.sent-2411-413: The circuits considered so far have only used loss correction and error detection at the very end, when interpreting the final measurements.
elements.sent-2411-414: However, it is important to be able to detect and correct errors between the steps of an algorithm, to enable deeper computation.
elements.sent-2411-415: To that end, we demonstrate repeated fault-tolerant loss correction.
elements.sent-2411-416: Repeated error correction, with higher-distance codes, has also been demonstrated on on trapped-ion processors~\cite{honeywell21steane, Postler23steaneec, Silva24microsoft12qubitcode, ReichardtMicrosoft24tesseract and superconducting processors~\cite{Krinner21repeatedsurfaceec,google23surface,google24surfacecode, amazon24catec .}}
elements.figure-2411-040: \subfigure[] {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=1]{images/zzzzsyndromenonft}}} \subfigure[\label{f:xxxxandzzzzsyndrome-no-erasure}] {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=1]{images/xxxxandzzzzsyndrome}}} \subfigure[\label{f:xxxxandzzzzsyndromeerasure}] {\raisebox{0cm}{\includegraphics[scale=1]{images/xxxxandzzzzsyndromeerasure}}} \caption{ {\bf a}, Circuit to measure the parity of four qubits, i.e., the syndrome of $ZZZZ$. It is not fault tolerant; a single $Z$ fault on the ancilla can spread undetected to a weight-two $Z$ error. {\bf b}, The next circuit remedies this, and simultaneously measures $XXXX$, using a trick from~\cite{Reichardt18steane}. Now the bad $Z$ fault will be detected, or ``flagged," by the $XXXX$ measurement, and similarly the $ZZZZ$ measurement will flag a bad $X$ fault. This gives an efficient, fault-tolerant error detection procedure for the $\llbracket 4,2,2 \rrbracket$ code. However, it does not correct for a lost qubit. {\bf c}, The last circuit fixes this, halfway, by switching the direction of the final two CNOT gates~\cite{McEwenBaconGidney23leakage, Chow24leakagedetectionunit}. The last two data qubits are replaced with fresh qubits, so in particular if one of them is lost it will be replaced. Thus all four qubits can be replaced in two rounds of error detection. } \label{f:xxxxandzzzzsyndrome}

(b)~The corresponding logical circuit. Since the CZ gates cancel out, both measurements should give~$+$. (c)~The baseline circuit run for comparison. The $X$ gates cancel out coherent dephasing errors from the CZ gates and movement. Both measurements should give $-$. This baseline performs better than applying the two CZ gates without leaving the interaction zone. } \label{f:repeatedec_czcz}

elements.sent-2411-431: In both protocols, we measure all the atoms of the prepared tesseract code blocks in the same $X$ or $Z$ basis, and then decode.
elements.sent-2411-432: Specifically, an encoded $|{+ 0{+ 0{+ 0 \rangle$ block can be either ``rejected'' due to $\ge2$ errors or losses detected, or ``correct'' if all the three deterministic values of logical $X$ or $Z$ operators are trivial, or ``wrong'' otherwise.}}}
elements.sent-2411-433: For the fault-tolerant protocol, we run 1980 and 2000 trials for $X$ and $Z$ basis, respectively; for the non--fault-tolerant protocol, we run 960 and 1000 trials, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-434: For each of these trials, we repeat the random pairing of the (accepted) blocks of encoded $|+\rangle ^{\otimes 3}$ independently for 100 shots.
elements.sent-2411-435: Results of these shots are shown in \figref{f:tesseractbar .}
elements.sent-2411-436: Specifically, we calculate the error rate, defined as the ratio of the number of wrong blocks over the number of correct or wrong blocks, both numbers summing over all shots of $X$ and $Z$ bases.
elements.sent-2411-437: We observe an error rate of $0.53(1)\%$ for the fault-tolerant protocol, nearly one order of magnitude smaller than $3.98(3)\%$ for the non--fault-tolerant protocol.
elements.sent-2411-438: However, the average number of correct blocks per shot is 3.85(1) for the fault-tolerant protocol, compared to 11.44(3) for the non--fault-tolerant protocol.
